title: STANDARD FOR HONEY CXS 12-1981 1 Adopted in 1981. Revised in 1987, 2001. Amended in 2019, 2022.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR HONEY CXS 12-1981 1 Adopted in 1981. Revised in 1987, 2001. Amended in 2019, 2022.
sections.summary: Document heading for the Codex Standard for Honey (CXS 12‑1981) with adoption/revision dates and a Secretariat note that further work was agreed on certain technical issues, particularly moisture content.

sections.section: 2022 Amendment The following amendment was made to the text of the standard following decisions taken at the forty-fifth session of the Codex Alimentarius Commission in December 2022.
sections.summary: Records the 2022 amendment replacing the previous non‑retail container labelling provision with a requirement to follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non‑Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346‑2021) and states the Annex is for voluntary application by commercial partners, not governments.

Defines scope: Part One covers all bee‑produced honeys intended for direct consumption in all presentation styles; Part Two covers honey for industrial use or packed for sale in bulk (which may be repacked into retail packs).
Heading only — Part One of the Standard (no substantive provisions in this heading).

Provides the definition of honey (natural sweet substance produced and transformed by honey bees) with subtypes blossom (nectar) and honeydew, and describes honey's composition (mainly sugars), variable colour, consistency (fluid to crystallised), and plant‑derived flavour and aroma.

Defines honey as the natural sweet substance produced by honey bees from plant nectar or secretions/excretions transformed and stored in the comb, and defines Blossom/Nectar honey (from nectar) and Honeydew honey (from insect excretions or plant secretions).

Describes honey composition—predominantly fructose and glucose with organic acids, enzymes and collection‑derived particles—and notes colour ranges, consistency from fluid to fully crystallised, and flavour/aroma derived from plant origin.

Sets essential composition and quality requirements: honey must contain only honey (no added food ingredients), be free of objectionable matter/taints/fermentation, not be excessively heated or chemically/biochemically treated to alter composition or crystallisation, and includes provisions for moisture and sugar content with specific limits for listed honey types.

sections.section: 3.1 Honey sold as such shall not have added to it any food ingredient, nor shall any other additions be made other than honey . Honey shall not have any objectionable matter , flavour, aroma, or taint absorbed from foreign matter during its processing and storage.  The honey shall not have begun to ferment or effervesce.  No pollen or constituent particular to honey may be removed except where this is unavoidable in the removal of foreign inorganic or organic matter.
sections.summary: Prohibits adding any food ingredient to honey sold as such, requires freedom from objectionable matter, taint or fermentation, and permits removal of pollen or honey constituents only when unavoidable to eliminate foreign inorganic or organic matter.
Identifies water‑insoluble solids content categories for honey styles, distinguishing (a) honeys other than pressed honey and (b) pressed honey, without numeric limits shown in this text.
States that no additives are permitted in honey.

Requires compliance with Codex maximum levels for contaminants: honey must be free from heavy metals at levels hazardous to human health and must comply with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides and veterinary drugs.
States that honey shall be free from heavy metals in amounts that may represent a hazard to human health and must comply with Codex‑established maximum levels for heavy metals.

sections.section: 5.2 Residues of Pesticides and Veterinary Drugs
sections.summary: Requires the products covered by this Standard to comply with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides and veterinary drugs in honey.

Recommends preparing and handling honey in accordance with the Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1‑1969) and other relevant Codex Codes, and complying with any microbiological criteria established under Codex guidelines.

sections.section: 6.1 It is recommended that the products covered by the provisions of this standard be prepared and handled in accordance  with  the  appropriate  sections  of  the General  Principles  of  Food  Hygiene (CXC  1-1969) recommended by the Codex Alimentarius Commission, and other relevant Codex texts such as Codes of Hygienic Practice and Codes of Practice.
sections.summary: Recommends that honey be prepared and handled in accordance with appropriate sections of the Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1‑1969) and relevant Codes of Hygienic Practice and Codes of Practice.

sections.section: 6.2 The products should comply with any microbiological criteria established in accordance with the Principles and Guidelines for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria Related to Foods (CXG 21-1997).
sections.summary: Products (honey) must comply with any microbiological criteria established pursuant to the Principles and Guidelines for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria Related to Foods (CXG 21-1997).
This labelling section supplements the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1-1985) and introduces specific additional labelling provisions for honey.

Sets naming requirements and allowed qualifiers for honey: products conforming to Part One must be called "honey," may be supplemented by terms like "blossom/nectar" or "honeydew" where appropriate, blends and floral or geographic designations are permitted only when the source conditions and organoleptic/physicochemical/microscopic properties correspond and the country of origin is declared, subsidiary designations require conformity, methods of removal (extracted/pressed/drained) are defined, and styles (liquid/crystalline, comb, cut comb) and filtered-honey labelling are specified.

sections.section: 7.1.11 Honey may be designated according to the following styles:
sections.summary: Permits three style designations for honey: (a) liquid or crystalline (or a mixture), (b) comb honey sold in sealed whole combs or sections, and (c) cut comb/chunk honey containing pieces of comb.

sections.section: 7.1.12 Honey which has been filtered in such a way as to result in the significant removal of pollen shall be designated filtered honey.
sections.summary: Honey that has been filtered so as to result in significant removal of pollen must be labelled "filtered honey."
Labelling of non-retail containers of honey should comply with the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).

Specifies methods and references for sampling and analysis of honey composition and quality, including AOAC and MAFF/Journal methods for sample preparation, moisture (AOAC 969.38B), sugar profiling and SCIRA for authenticity (AOAC 977.20 and 991.41), and MAFF V22 for water‑insoluble solids, while noting some methods are to be finalized and that a screening method for cane-sugar adulteration exists.
Sample preparation for honey analysis must follow AOAC 920.180.
Moisture content in honey is to be determined using AOAC 969.38B (and equivalent MAFF validated method V21 / J. Assoc. Public Analysts references).
Heading indicating determination of sugars in honey with subsections for fructose+glucose (sum) and sucrose content; specific methods are not detailed here.

sections.section: 8.3.1 Fructose and Glucose Content (sum of both)
sections.summary: Non‑substantive subsection heading for determination of the combined fructose and glucose content in honey.
Non‑substantive subsection heading for determination of sucrose content in honey.
Water‑insoluble solids in honey are to be determined using the J. Assoc. Public Analysts (1992) method / MAFF validated method V22.
Subsection heading for determination of electrical conductivity in honey; specific method references are noted elsewhere as to be finalized.

sections.section: 8.6 Determination of sugars added to honey (authenticity)
sections.summary: Methods for detecting added sugars in honey are specified, including AOAC 977.20 for sugar profile and AOAC 991.41 (SCIRA) for isotope analysis, with a note that a screening method for cane-sugar adulteration is available and some method details remain to be finalized.

Annex (voluntary, intended for commercial partners, not governments) defines additional compositional/quality factors for honey (free acidity, diastase activity, HMF limits with tropical exception, and electrical conductivity categories) and provides detailed methods and literature references for their determination, and notes that honey for industrial uses remains under consideration.

Lists additional composition and quality factors for honey: free acidity, diastase activity, hydroxymethylfurfural content (with tropical exception), and categories/exceptions for electrical conductivity.
Free acidity of honey shall not exceed 50 milliequivalents of acid per 1000 g (per kg).
Diastase activity of honey, after processing/blending, shall generally be not less than 8 Schade units, with a lower limit of 3 Schade units for honeys with naturally low enzyme content.
Hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) in honey after processing/blending shall not exceed 40 mg/kg, except for honey declared from tropical regions (and blends thereof) where the limit is 80 mg/kg.

Classifies honeys for electrical conductivity into three groups: (a) honeys not in (b) or (c) and their blends; (b) honeydew and chestnut honeys and their blends except when blended with those in (c); and (c) lists specific floral exceptions (Strawberry tree, Bell Heather, Eucalyptus, Lime/Tilia, Ling Heather, Manuka/Jelly bush, Tea tree).

Introduces the methods of sampling and analysis for additional compositional and quality factors in Section 1, referencing sample preparation (section 7.1), specific analytical methods for acidity, diastase and HMF, and accompanying literature references.
Specifies that sample preparation follows section 7.1 of the Standard and requires samples for diastase activity and HMF determinations to be prepared without heating.
Lists the referenced analytical methods: acidity by J. Assoc. Public Analysts (1992)/MAFF validated method V19, diastase activity by AOAC 958.09, and HMF content by AOAC 980.23.
Specifies the determination of acidity in honey using the J. Assoc. Public Analysts (1992) method and MAFF validated method V19.
Specifies AOAC 958.09 as the method for determining diastase activity in honey.
Specifies AOAC 980.23 as the method for determining hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) content in honey.

Provides a bibliography of literature and standards relevant to diastase and HMF determination methods, interlaboratory trials and harmonised methods for honey, noting CCS asked CCMAS to consider retaining only essential references.
